senses.definition: A genus of small, spiny, cushion‑forming plants found mainly in dry, rocky regions of Eurasia.
examples: Acantholimon species are often used as rock‑garden plants by specialist gardeners.
The botanist collected several Acantholimon samples from the mountain slopes.

senses.definition: Relating to the study of spines or thorny structures, especially in plants or animals.
examples: The researcher published an acanthological study of desert shrubs.
Her acanthological work focuses on the evolution of cactus spines.

senses.definition: A branch of biology that deals with spines or thorny structures in plants or animals.
examples: Acanthology can shed light on how spines help plants survive in dry climates.
The museum holds several classic works in acanthology.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which the cells in a layer of the skin lose contact with each other and break apart.
examples: Acantholysis is a key feature seen under the microscope in some blistering skin diseases.
The biopsy confirmed acantholysis in the epidermis.

senses.definition: A tumour of the skin that grows from cells in the prickly (spiny) layer of the epidermis.
examples: The dermatologist removed a benign acanthoma from the patient’s leg.
Clear cell acanthoma is usually seen as a small, slow‑growing red nodule on the skin.

senses.definition: Plural of acanthoma: tumours of the skin that arise from cells in the prickly layer of the epidermis.
examples: Several acanthomas were identified on the patient’s trunk.
These rare acanthomas are usually benign and slow growing.

senses.definition: An old or specialised scientific name for a family or group of spiny organisms, probably used in invertebrate zoology.
examples: The family Acanthomeridae is mentioned only in older zoological texts.
Taxonomists no longer commonly use the name Acanthomeridae in modern classifications.

senses.definition: A spine or spiny process on an animal, especially in older zoological writing.
examples: The fish’s dorsal acanthon is clearly visible in the diagram.
Each acanthon on the insect’s leg helps it cling to rough surfaces.

senses.definition: A former or alternative botanical genus name for certain spiny shrubs and small trees, now usually included in the genus Eleutherococcus.
examples: Acanthopanax species are cultivated in some regions as ornamental shrubs.
The herbal extract once sold as Acanthopanax is now labelled under a different genus.

senses.definition: A genus of highly venomous Australian snakes known as death adders, with a short, thick body and a tail ending in a spine.
examples: Acanthophis species are responsible for some serious snakebites in Australia.
The common death adder belongs to the genus Acanthophis.

senses.definition: Bearing spines or thorn‑like projections.
examples: The acanthophorous stems protect the plant from grazing animals.
Many desert plants are acanthophorous, reducing water loss and discouraging herbivores.

senses.definition: An animal or limb that has spiny feet or legs, especially in specialised zoological descriptions.
examples: The insect is an acanthopod with rows of spines along each leg.
Several acanthopod species live among the rocky shorelines.

senses.definition: Having legs or feet that are spiny or covered with sharp projections.
examples: The acanthopodous larvae cling tightly to submerged plants.
Several acanthopodous crustaceans were found in the sample.

senses.definition: Having a mouth or oral region that is armed with spines.
examples: An acanthopomatous jaw helps the fish grip slippery prey.
The species is acanthopomatous, with small spines surrounding the mouth opening.

senses.definition: A small, spine‑like opening or tube on the surface of certain fossil bryozoans, visible in microscopic study.
examples: The presence of acanthopores helps to identify this bryozoan genus.
Under magnification, each acanthopore appears as a tiny spine‑bearing tube.

senses.definition: Relating to a group of bony fishes that have spiny rays at the front of the dorsal and other fins.
examples: Many acanthopteran fishes inhabit coral reefs.
Acanthopteran species typically have a mix of spiny and soft fin rays.

senses.definition: A fish belonging to the group of spiny‑rayed bony fishes.
examples: The perch is a familiar acanthopteran found in freshwater lakes.
Several acanthopterans were collected during the trawl survey.

senses.definition: An old taxonomic order or group name for spiny‑rayed bony fishes.
examples: In older classifications, many familiar fishes were placed in the Acanthopteri.
The term Acanthopteri is now largely replaced by more detailed groups in modern ichthyology.

senses.definition: Having spiny or stiff rays in the fins, especially referring to certain fishes.
examples: Acanthopterous fishes often have a row of sharp spines along the front of the dorsal fin.
The acanthopterous form of the fin offers extra protection from predators.

senses.definition: Belonging to or characteristic of the Acanthopterygii, a large group of spiny‑rayed bony fishes.
examples: Many acanthopterygian fish species are important in commercial fisheries.
Acanthopterygian features include a spiny dorsal fin and specialised jaw structures.

senses.definition: A fish belonging to the group Acanthopterygii.
examples: Perches and wrasses are typical acanthopterygians.
The reef community is dominated by acanthopterygians and a few soft‑rayed species.

senses.definition: A large group or superorder of bony fishes that have spiny rays in the front part of their fins.
examples: The Acanthopterygii include many common marine and freshwater fishes.
Modern classifications still use Acanthopterygii as a broad grouping of spiny‑rayed fishes.

senses.definition: Plural of acanthosis: conditions where the top layer of the skin becomes thicker than normal.
examples: Different acanthoses can be seen in patients with chronic skin irritation.
The dermatologist compared several acanthoses on the patient’s arms.

senses.definition: A thickening of the prickle‑cell layer of the skin, often seen in certain rashes and chronic skin diseases.
examples: Acanthosis is common in long‑standing eczema.
Microscopic examination revealed marked acanthosis and inflammation.

senses.definition: Showing or related to acanthosis, with a thickened prickle‑cell layer of the skin.
examples: The biopsy showed acanthotic epidermis with mild inflammation.
Acanthotic changes were present around the chronic wound.

senses.definition: Having spines, thorns, or spine‑like structures.
examples: The acanthous leaves protect the plant from being eaten.
Many acanthous marine animals live on rocky reefs.

senses.definition: Plural of acanth, a rare term for a spine or thorn‑like structure.
examples: Numerous acanths project from the shell’s surface.
The fossil’s acanths are easily seen under low magnification.

senses.definition: A family of marine fishes, including surgeonfishes and tangs, known for the sharp spine on each side of the tail.
examples: Members of the Acanthuridae are common on tropical coral reefs.
The distinctive tail spine is a key feature of the Acanthuridae.

senses.definition: A genus of marine fishes in the family Acanthuridae, commonly called surgeonfishes or tangs.
examples: Acanthurus species are popular in marine aquariums.
The blue tang belongs to the genus Acanthurus.

senses.definition: A plant with large, often spiny leaves and tall spikes of white or purple flowers, found mainly in warm regions.
examples: Acanthus grows well in sunny, well‑drained gardens.
The glossy, spiny leaves of the acanthus stand out against the wall.

senses.definition: A carved decorative pattern based on the leaves of the acanthus plant, especially used on classical columns.
examples: The Corinthian capitals were decorated with detailed acanthus leaves.
Acanthus motifs are common in classical and baroque architecture.

senses.definition: Plural of acanthus: several plants of the acanthus type or several acanthus‑style ornamental designs.
examples: A row of acanthuses lined the edge of the garden.
The frieze shows acanthuses and other stylised leaves.

senses.definition: An uncommon or obsolete taxonomic name, probably referring to a group of spiny‑tailed fishes.
examples: The name Acanthuthi appears only in older zoological references.
Modern classifications no longer use Acanthuthi as a valid group.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which the amount of carbon dioxide in the blood is abnormally low.
examples: Hyperventilation can lead to acapnia and dizziness.
Arterial blood tests confirmed acapnia in the patient.

senses.definition: Relating to or affected by acapnia, having abnormally low carbon dioxide in the blood.
examples: The patient entered an acapnial state after prolonged hyperventilation.
Acapnial conditions can change blood pH significantly.

senses.definition: Plural of acapnia: states in which the blood has an abnormally low level of carbon dioxide.
examples: Various acapnias can occur in patients with anxiety disorders.
The study examined different acapnias induced by altered ventilation.

senses.definition: Using only voices, without any musical instruments (non‑standard spelling of “a cappella”).
examples: The choir sang the hymn acappella.
They performed the song acappella in the echoing hall.

senses.definition: Describing music that is sung without instruments (non‑standard spelling of “a cappella”).
examples: They released an acappella album of traditional songs.
Her acappella track went viral online.

senses.definition: Lacking a capsule or outer covering, especially referring to organs, joints, or bacteria.
examples: Acapsular bacteria may be less resistant to the host’s immune system.
The acapsular joint surface was directly exposed in the injury.

senses.definition: A tropical South American tree with hard wood used in construction, or the wood from this tree.
examples: The beams were made of durable acapu.
Acapu is valued locally for its resistance to decay.

senses.definition: A coastal city and major tourist resort in southwestern Mexico, on the Pacific coast.
examples: They spent their honeymoon in Acapulco, enjoying the beaches.
Acapulco is famous for its cliff divers and nightlife.

senses.definition: Any of several small to medium‑sized cichlid fishes from tropical American rivers, some of which are kept in aquariums.
examples: The aquarium contains a colourful acara from the Amazon.
Many hobbyists keep acara species with other peaceful fish.

senses.definition: A genus of microscopic mites that infest honeybees and can cause disease.
examples: Acarapis mites can weaken bee colonies by damaging their breathing tubes.
The presence of Acarapis in the hive was confirmed under the microscope.

senses.definition: Any of several toucans of the genus Pteroglossus from tropical America, also known as araçaris.
examples: The zoo recently received an acarari from Brazil.
Brightly coloured acararis feed mainly on fruit.

senses.definition: An oral medicine used to treat type 2 diabetes by slowing the digestion of carbohydrates in the gut.
examples: Acarbose is taken with meals to reduce post‑meal rises in blood sugar.
The doctor adjusted the dose of acarbose to improve glucose control.

senses.definition: Plural of acarbose: different forms or doses of the drug acarbose.
examples: The study compared various acarboses and their side‑effect profiles.
Generic acarboses have become widely available.

senses.definition: A rare congenital condition in which a fetus develops without a functioning heart.
examples: Acardia occurs most often in twin pregnancies.
The ultrasound findings were consistent with acardia in one twin.

senses.definition: Having no heart or lacking a functioning heart, especially describing a malformed fetus.
examples: An acardiac twin depends on blood flow from the healthy co‑twin.
Acardiac fetuses cannot survive outside the womb.

senses.definition: A very rare or obsolete term for inflammation involving the heart or the region around it.
examples: The term acardite appears in older medical texts but is seldom used today.
Acardite was once applied to severe inflammatory heart conditions.

senses.definition: A major group (subclass) of small arachnids that includes mites and ticks.
examples: The Acari contain thousands of species living in many habitats.
Medical entomology often focuses on Acari that transmit disease.

senses.definition: Relating to mites or ticks.
examples: Acarian parasites can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions.
The acarian fauna of the soil is surprisingly rich.

senses.definition: A mite or tick.
examples: Several acarians were found on the bird’s feathers.
The soil sample contained numerous acarians.

senses.definition: Plural of acariasis: diseases in humans or animals caused by infestation with mites or ticks.
examples: Various acariases affect livestock around the world.
The book chapter reviews common acariases in domestic animals.

senses.definition: Disease or irritation caused by infestation with mites or ticks.
examples: Acariasis can lead to intense itching and skin lesions.
Farmers must monitor animals for signs of acariasis.

senses.definition: A specialist who studies or treats diseases caused by mites and ticks.
examples: The acariatre identified the species of mite responsible for the outbreak.
In some regions, an acariatre works closely with veterinarians.
